clues: The Ebers Papyrus, one of the oldest surviving medical texts from ancient Egypt from 1550 BC, describes uses of the autumn crocus to treat pain and swelling. This medication was later derived from this plant.

In Greek mythology, the autumn crocus was said to have sprung from the blood of the chained Titan Prometheus. Medea, a powerful sorceress, used the autumn crocus as one of her poisons as this substance binds to tubulin to suppress cell division and has a narrow therapeutic window.
A versatile anti-inflammatory medication, this is used in Familial Mediterranean Fever, pericarditis, and most commonly gouty arthritis

Women are more likely to be affected by this disease and historically, women who were affected were dismissed as hysterical or emotionally unstable. Dr. Wolff, in his 1948 publication concluded that this disease 'represents a collapse of a way of dealing with life situations which are stressful to the individual'
There are numerous potential triggers including changes in barometric pressure, alcohol, chocolate, and strong smells
An episode typically follows this sequence: prodrome, aura, headache, postdrome
In a clear ethics violation, researchers fed this to 20 prison inmates in the Philippines to prove the disease's transmission. Some developed acute diarrhea while others were asymptomatic

This ravaged US and allied forces in tropical regions such as the South Pacific and North Africa during WWII. Because of the disease's long incubation period, many service members returned home to the US only to experience recurrent symptoms later
While this parasite typically stays in the large intestine, it can also cause an anchovy paste-like abscesss to form when it travels to the liver
For decades, scientists believed that proteins carried our genetic information. Experiments involving this bacteria were pivotal in helping us understand that DNA is the carrier
Prior to the discovery of penicillin

The Gram stain was created in 1882 by Hans Christian Gram for the sole purpose of viewing this bacteria in human lung tissue. At that time, existing stains colored human cells and bacterial cells the exact same shade.
Prior to the discovery of penicillin, this diplococci was famously dubbed the 'Captain of the Men of Death' by Sir William Osler in 1918 due to high associated mortality

Dating back to at least the 1500s in China, practitioners took scabs and dried them. This was then ground into a powder and blown into healthy patients' noses using a silver tube in a early version of this practice
One of the most successful public health interventions, this is credited with saving over 154 million lives globally in the last 50 years

While you may have heard of Edward Jenners observations of local milkmaids, did you know that the life-saving practice was actually brought to the American colonies more than 50 years earlier by an enslaved African man, who explained that rubbing pus from an affected person into an open wound of an uninfected individual would confer immunity
